masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through American History, Vol. 2
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through American History, Vol. 2
masthead.dek: A fresh round from the colonies to the modern era — routed to a state, a decade, a count, a battle, or an amendment, never just 'which president.' Each one sets up the obvious wrong instinct, then asks for the real answer. Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Built fresh against the existing American-history set — no repeated questions. Verified to the National Archives, Library of Congress, NPS, Mount Vernon, and the constitution annotated.

plan.note: Volume 2: deliberately routes off the first set's president-heavy axes onto states, decades, counts, battles/treaties, and amendments, each with a reflex-defeater.

questions.q: Twelve of the thirteen colonies sent delegates to the First Continental Congress in 1774. Which one sent none at all?
questions.detail: The only one of the thirteen colonies that sent no delegates to the First Continental Congress in 1774.
questions.why: Reflex says a small or distant New England colony skipped it, but every northern colony showed up. The youngest colony, dependent on British troops against a frontier conflict, stayed home.
questions.more: Georgia did join the Second Continental Congress the following year.
questions.source: Mount Vernon, 'First Continental Congress' (mountvernon.org); Britannica, 'Continental Congress'

questions.q: Which of the thirteen states refused to send any delegate at all to the 1787 Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia?
questions.detail: The only one of the thirteen states that refused to send any delegate to the 1787 convention in Philadelphia.
questions.why: People guess a big, suspicious state, but it was the smallest one, fearful of a strong central government, that boycotted the whole convention.
questions.more: It was also the last of the thirteen to ratify, holding out until 1790.
questions.source: History.com, '7 Things You May Not Know About the Constitutional Convention'; RI Secretary of State, 'RI and the US Constitution'

questions.q: The Declaration of Independence and the Constitution feel like one founding cast, but they were signed eleven years apart. How many men signed both documents?
questions.detail: The number of men who signed both the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution.
questions.why: The two documents feel like one founding cast, so people guess high. Eleven years and different delegations apart, only a handful bridged both.
questions.more: The six were Franklin, Robert Morris, George Read, Roger Sherman, James Wilson, and George Clymer.
questions.source: National Constitution Center, 'About the Signers'; Heritage Foundation, signers overview

questions.q: On the final day of the Constitutional Convention, how many delegates who were still present in the room refused to sign the document?
questions.detail: The number of delegates still present on the final day who refused to sign the Constitution.
questions.why: The signing reads as unanimous in memory, but three holdouts at the table said no, two of them from the same state as its chief architect.
questions.more: They were Edmund Randolph and George Mason of Virginia and Elbridge Gerry of Massachusetts.
questions.source: Teaching American History, 'Gerry, Mason, and Randolph Decline to Sign'; American Battlefield Trust, 'Who Signed and Who Did Not'

questions.q: The old Articles of Confederation required all states to agree. Under the Constitution itself, how many state ratifications were needed before it would take effect?
questions.detail: The number of state ratifications the Constitution itself required before it would take effect.
questions.why: People assume all thirteen had to agree, like under the old Articles. The drafters deliberately set the bar below unanimity so a few holdouts could not block it.
questions.more: New Hampshire became the ninth in June 1788, triggering the new government.
questions.source: Article VII, U.S. Constitution (constitution.congress.gov); National Constitution Center

questions.q: We remember the Bill of Rights as ten amendments, but Congress actually sent more than that to the states in 1789. How many amendments did Congress send?
questions.detail: The number of amendments Congress sent to the states in 1789, of which ten became the Bill of Rights.
questions.why: The 'Bill of Rights' is fixed at ten in memory, but Congress actually shipped more; two failed to clear the states at the time.
questions.more: One of the two leftovers was finally ratified in 1992 as the 27th Amendment.
questions.source: National Archives, 'Bill of Rights'; National Constitution Center, 'Why didn't the original 12 amendments make it'

questions.q: Fifty-five delegates attended the Constitutional Convention, but most were gone or refused by the end. How many signatures were actually on the Constitution when the convention closed?
questions.detail: The number of signatures on the Constitution at the close of the convention.
questions.why: Fifty-five delegates attended, so people guess in the fifties. Most had gone home or balked by signing day, leaving a far smaller number.
questions.more: One man even signed on behalf of an absent colleague from Delaware.
questions.source: National Archives, 'The Constitution: How Did it Happen?'; History.com, 'U.S. Constitution signed'

questions.q: James Madison is called the 'Father of the Constitution,' but a different writer penned more than half of the 85 Federalist essays nearly single-handedly. Who wrote the largest share?
questions.detail: The author of the largest share of the Federalist essays, roughly 51 of the 85.
questions.why: Madison is remembered as 'Father of the Constitution,' so people credit him, but a different Publius wrote more than half the essays nearly single-handed.
questions.more: Madison and John Jay wrote the rest, Jay only a handful.
questions.source: Library of Congress, 'About the Authors' (Federalist); Britannica, 'The Federalist'

questions.q: At just 26 years old, who was the youngest man to sign the Declaration of Independence?
questions.detail: At 26, the youngest man to sign the Declaration of Independence.
questions.why: People reach for a famous name, but the youngest signer was a South Carolina lawyer who had actually argued against declaring independence days before voting for it.
questions.more: He later became governor of South Carolina.
questions.source: National Constitution Center, 'Edward Rutledge'; American History Central

questions.q: Colonial religious refuge usually brings the Puritans to mind, but which Chesapeake colony was founded as a haven for persecuted English Catholics?
questions.detail: The colony founded in 1634 as a refuge for English Catholics.
questions.why: People associate colonial religious refuge with Puritans up north, but this Chesapeake colony was the one chartered for a persecuted Catholic minority.
questions.more: Its 1649 Toleration Act was an early law protecting Christian worship.
questions.source: Bill of Rights Institute, 'The Founding of Maryland'; History.com, 'The settlement of Maryland'

questions.q: Three small river towns adopted a written frame of government based on the people's consent, often called the first written constitution in the colonies. In which colony?
questions.detail: The colony whose 1639 Fundamental Orders are often called the first written constitution in the colonies.
questions.why: People credit a big, famous colony, but it was three small river towns that adopted a written frame of government grounded in the consent of the people.
questions.more: The document is why the state is nicknamed the Constitution State.
questions.source: History.com, 'The first colonial constitution'; Connecticut History, 'The Fundamental Orders'

questions.q: People assume Massachusetts or Virginia led the way, but which colony was the first to formally authorize its delegates to vote for independence, nearly three months before the July vote?
questions.detail: In April 1776 it became the first colony to formally authorize its delegates to vote for independence.
questions.why: People assume Massachusetts or Virginia led, but a southern colony's Halifax Resolves beat the July vote by nearly three months.
questions.more: Virginia followed in May with its own instruction to vote for independence.
questions.source: NCpedia, 'The Halifax Resolves'; ShareAmerica, 'North Carolina's pioneering push'

questions.q: Washington was inaugurated and the first government met under the new Constitution in which city, not Philadelphia and not the capital that did not yet exist?
questions.detail: The first national capital under the new Constitution, where Washington was inaugurated in 1789.
questions.why: People say Philadelphia, the convention city, or Washington, which did not yet exist. The first government actually met and inaugurated in a different city entirely.
questions.more: Philadelphia then served as the temporary capital through 1800.
questions.source: White House Historical Association, 'The Nation's First Capital: New York City'; Library of Congress

questions.q: The Revolution is bracketed by 1776 and the 1783 peace treaty, but in what year did the decisive battle at Yorktown, where Cornwallis surrendered, take place?
questions.detail: The year of Yorktown, the last major battle of the Revolution, where Cornwallis surrendered.
questions.why: People anchor on 1776 or the 1783 peace treaty, but the decisive battle that effectively ended the fighting came in between.
questions.more: A French fleet and army were essential to trapping the British on the Virginia peninsula.
questions.source: NPS, 'Yorktown Battlefield'; Britannica, 'Siege of Yorktown'

questions.q: People place the first national government alongside the 1776 Declaration, but in which decade did the Articles of Confederation finally take effect after years of ratification delays?
questions.detail: The decade the Articles of Confederation finally took effect, in 1781.
questions.why: People place the first national government in the 1770s alongside the Declaration, but unanimous ratification dragged on for years past 1777.
questions.more: Maryland's holdout over western land claims delayed the whole thing.
questions.source: House History, 'The Articles of Confederation'; Mount Vernon

questions.q: People cluster colonial unrest right before independence, but in which decade did Parliament pass the Stamp Act that sparked 'no taxation without representation'?
questions.detail: The decade Parliament passed the Stamp Act, the tax that sparked 'no taxation without representation.'
questions.why: People mentally cluster colonial unrest right before 1776, but the tax that ignited organized protest came a full decade earlier.
questions.more: Parliament repealed it within a year under colonial boycott pressure.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Stamp Act'; National Constitution Center, 'On This Day: The Stamp Act'

questions.q: The Central Pacific started in Sacramento and the Union Pacific near Omaha, but in which state did the two halves of the first transcontinental railroad actually meet at the golden spike?
questions.detail: The two halves of the first transcontinental railroad were joined with a golden spike at Promontory Summit on May 10, 1869.
questions.why: Reflex says California (the Central Pacific started in Sacramento) or Nebraska (the Union Pacific started near Omaha) — but the rails actually met in the middle, in Utah Territory, north of the Great Salt Lake.
questions.more: A single telegraphed word, 'done,' announced the link to the nation.
questions.source: Library of Congress, Golden Spike research guide (guides.loc.gov/chronicling-america-golden-spike); Utah History to Go

questions.q: The Union Pacific laid its first transcontinental track westward from Omaha. In which state did that eastern construction terminus sit, given Council Bluffs across the river is in Iowa?
questions.detail: The Union Pacific laid its first transcontinental track westward from Omaha, the railroad's eastern starting point on the Missouri River.
questions.why: People picture the line beginning in the East Coast cities that funded it; the actual eastern construction terminus was the frontier town of Omaha, Nebraska — Council Bluffs across the river is in Iowa.
questions.more: The two riverbank towns weren't physically bridged by rail until 1872.
questions.source: Wikipedia, First transcontinental railroad; Union Pacific (uprr.com)

questions.q: Pickett's Charge was led by Virginians under a Virginia general, but in which state did that failed assault, the 'high-water mark of the Confederacy,' actually take place?
questions.detail: Pickett's Charge, the failed Confederate assault on July 3, 1863, marked the 'high-water mark of the Confederacy' at Gettysburg.
questions.why: The charge was led by Virginians under a Virginia general, so 'Virginia' is the trap; the assault and the war's turning point happened on Pennsylvania soil.
questions.more: It pitted roughly 12,000 Confederates against half that many defenders on Cemetery Ridge.
questions.source: American Battlefield Trust, Pickett's Charge; Wikipedia, High-water mark of the Confederacy

questions.q: Grant's siege that split the Confederacy and won the Union full control of the Mississippi River ended at Vicksburg. In which state is Vicksburg?
questions.detail: Grant's 47-day siege ended with the Confederate surrender of Vicksburg on July 4, 1863, giving the Union full control of the Mississippi River.
questions.why: The battle that 'split the Confederacy along the river' sounds like it should be in a border state; it was at Vicksburg, in the state of Mississippi itself.
questions.more: It fell the day after Gettysburg, a one-two blow to the Confederacy.
questions.source: Britannica, Vicksburg Campaign; American Battlefield Trust

questions.q: Many guess Virginia or the capital area, but in which state, the first to secede, did Confederate guns fire on Fort Sumter to open the Civil War?
questions.detail: Confederate guns opened the Civil War by firing on Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor on April 12, 1861.
questions.why: Many guess Virginia (where most big battles followed) or the new capital area; the very first shots were fired in South Carolina, the first state to secede.
questions.more: The fort surrendered after a 34-hour bombardment with no battle deaths.
questions.source: Britannica, Battle of Fort Sumter; U.S. Senate history

questions.q: Custer's famous fight pulls people toward Montana, but in which state, on the Pine Ridge reservation, did the Wounded Knee Massacre, the last major clash of the Indian Wars, occur?
questions.detail: The Wounded Knee Massacre on December 29, 1890, the last major clash of the Indian Wars, took place along Wounded Knee Creek.
questions.why: Custer's famous fight pulls people toward Montana; the LAST major confrontation was farther east, in South Dakota, on the Pine Ridge reservation.
questions.more: It followed the killing of Sitting Bull two weeks earlier.
questions.source: Britannica, Wounded Knee Massacre; History.com

questions.q: 'Custer's Last Stand' feels like Dakota or Wyoming country to many, but in which state did the Battle of the Little Bighorn actually take place?
questions.detail: Custer and roughly 200 of his men were killed at the Battle of the Little Bighorn on June 25, 1876.
questions.why: 'Custer's Last Stand' feels like Dakota or Wyoming Sioux country to many; the battle was in southern Montana Territory.
questions.more: Up to 3,000 Lakota and Cheyenne warriors overwhelmed his battalion in about an hour.
questions.source: Britannica, Battle of the Little Bighorn; History.com

questions.q: People expect a progressive Northeastern state, but which frontier state was the first where women could vote, granting them full voting rights as a territory decades before the 19th Amendment?
questions.detail: Wyoming Territory granted women full voting rights in 1869 and kept the law on becoming a state in 1890 — the first U.S. state where women could vote.
questions.why: People expect a progressive Northeastern state like Massachusetts; the frontier territory of Wyoming led the world here, decades before the 19th Amendment.
questions.more: Louisa Swain cast the first ballot under the law in Laramie in 1870.
questions.source: WyoHistory.org; History.com, 'The State Where Women Voted'

questions.q: The Trail of Tears is remembered by where it started in Georgia and the Southeast, but the Cherokee were forced west to Indian Territory in what present-day state?
questions.detail: The Cherokee were forced west along the Trail of Tears in 1838–39 to Indian Territory, in present-day Oklahoma.
questions.why: The route is remembered by where it started — Georgia and the Southeast — but the DESTINATION was the land that became Oklahoma.
questions.more: An estimated 4,000–5,000 Cherokee died on the roughly 800-mile journey.
questions.source: Britannica, Trail of Tears; National Archives, Cherokee Removal

questions.q: The crowds of 'forty-niners' arrived in 1849, but the gold strike at Sutter's Mill that set off the Gold Rush happened the year before, in which state?
questions.detail: James Marshall's January 1848 find at Sutter's Mill set off the Gold Rush; the largest wave of 'forty-niners' arrived in 1849.
questions.why: Easy to place, but the slicer is the date: the gold was actually found in 1848 — the '49ers' are named for when the crowds arrived, not the discovery.
questions.more: The boom fast-tracked California to statehood in 1850.
questions.source: Britannica, California Gold Rush; Smithsonian Magazine

questions.q: People link the Erie Canal to opening the Midwest and reach for an Ohio or Great Lakes state, but the entire 363-mile canal lies within which single state?
questions.detail: The Erie Canal, completed in 1825, linked the Hudson River at Albany to Lake Erie at Buffalo.
questions.why: People associate the canal with opening the Midwest, so they reach for an Ohio or Great Lakes state; the whole 363-mile canal lies within New York.
questions.more: It cut shipping costs from about $100 to $6 per ton and cemented New York City's commercial dominance.
questions.source: U.S. Capitol Visitor Center; Wikipedia, Erie Canal

questions.q: The huge Mexican Cession feels like it belongs alongside the 1850s slavery compromises, but in which decade was the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, ending the Mexican-American War, signed?
questions.detail: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, signed February 2, 1848, ended the Mexican-American War and handed the U.S. over 525,000 square miles.
questions.why: The huge Mexican Cession feels like a 1850s milestone alongside the slavery compromises, but the treaty was signed in 1848 — the 1840s.
questions.more: The land became all or part of seven future states.
questions.source: Britannica, Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo; National Archives

questions.q: Alaska feels like a modern frontier, so people guess late, but in which decade, just after the Civil War, did the U.S. buy Alaska from Russia in the deal mocked as 'Seward's Folly'?
questions.detail: Seward negotiated the purchase of Alaska from Russia for $7.2 million in 1867, mocked at the time as 'Seward's Folly.'
questions.why: Alaska feels like a modern frontier, so people guess later; the deal was actually struck in 1867, just after the Civil War.
questions.more: Ridicule faded after the 1896 Klondike gold strike.
questions.source: Britannica, Alaska Purchase; Library of Congress

questions.q: People tie the Statue of Liberty to the 1876 centennial that inspired it or to the 1890s immigration peak, but in which decade was the statue itself dedicated in New York Harbor?
questions.detail: The Statue of Liberty, a gift from France, was dedicated in New York Harbor on October 28, 1886.
questions.why: People tie it to the 1876 centennial that inspired it, or to Ellis Island's 1890s immigration peak; the statue itself was dedicated in 1886.
questions.more: President Grover Cleveland presided over the dedication.
questions.source: History.com, Statue of Liberty dedicated; National Park Service

questions.q: This legendary symbol of the West feels like it lasted years, but how long did the Pony Express actually operate before the telegraph made it obsolete?
questions.detail: The Pony Express ran from April 3, 1860, to October 26, 1861 — roughly 18 months.
questions.why: Such a legendary symbol of the West feels like it lasted years; the transcontinental telegraph made it obsolete almost at once.
questions.more: It closed just two days after the telegraph line was completed.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Pony Express; U.S. Postal Service facts (facts.usps.com)

questions.q: People remember the Homestead Act offered 'free land' but forget the size of a standard claim. How many acres did a settler get for farming and living on it for five years?
questions.detail: The Homestead Act of 1862 offered 160 acres of public land to settlers who farmed and lived on it for five years.
questions.why: People remember 'free land' but not the size; a quarter-section — 160 acres — was the standard claim, not 40 or a whole square mile.
questions.more: The law ultimately distributed about 270 million acres before the last Alaska claim in 1986.
questions.source: National Archives, Homestead Act; Britannica

questions.q: The Missouri Compromise is remembered for pairing Missouri and Maine, but its real point was the resulting Senate balance. How many free states and how many slave states did the Union then hold?
questions.detail: After the Missouri Compromise of 1820, the Union held 12 free states and 12 slave states — a deliberate balance.
questions.why: The compromise is remembered for one pairing (Missouri and Maine), but its point was the resulting tally: an even 12-to-12 split in the Senate.
questions.more: It barred slavery in Louisiana Purchase lands north of the 36°30′ line.
questions.source: National Archives, Missouri Compromise; World History Encyclopedia

questions.q: People know a cluster of Western states entered the Union together around 1889–1890 but rarely the count. How many states were admitted in that burst?
questions.detail: Six states were admitted in 1889–1890: North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, Washington (1889) and Idaho, Wyoming (1890).
questions.why: People know a cluster of Western states came in together but rarely the number; an enabling act and follow-ons added six in barely two years.
questions.more: North and South Dakota entered the same day, their order deliberately obscured.
questions.source: Congress.gov, Admission of States; HistoryLink.org

questions.q: The last land the U.S. bought from Mexico sounds like it should touch California or Texas, but the Gadsden Purchase actually rounded out which two of today's states?
questions.detail: The 1853–54 Gadsden Purchase added a strip of land south of the Gila River to what is now southern Arizona and New Mexico.
questions.why: The last contiguous-U.S. land buy from Mexico sounds like it should touch California or Texas; it actually rounded out only Arizona and New Mexico.
questions.more: It was bought largely to clear a route for a southern transcontinental railroad.
questions.source: Britannica, Gadsden Purchase; U.S. State Department Office of the Historian

questions.q: People remember a 'line' divided slave and free territory under the Missouri Compromise but not where it ran. It was not the Mason-Dixon line, so which parallel was it?
questions.detail: The Missouri Compromise barred slavery in Louisiana Purchase territory north of the 36°30′ parallel.
questions.why: People remember a 'line' divided slave and free territory but not where; it was the 36°30′ parallel, matching Missouri's southern border — not the Mason-Dixon line.
questions.more: The Kansas-Nebraska Act effectively repealed this line in 1854.
questions.source: National Archives, Missouri Compromise; World History Encyclopedia

questions.q: After a wartime Supreme Court ruling let states set one voting age for federal elections and another for their own, which amendment, ratified in about 100 days, fixed that split nationwide?
questions.detail: An amendment was ratified in under four months, the fastest ever, after a wartime Supreme Court ruling left states able to set one voting age for federal elections and another for their own. It fixed that split nationwide.
questions.why: The reflex is to think of the most famous voting-rights amendments. But this is the speed-and-uniformity clue: the 26th, lowering the voting age to 18, ratified July 1, 1971, in about 100 days.
questions.more: Congress acted after Oregon v. Mitchell (1970) said Congress could set 18 for federal but not state elections, creating chaos.
questions.source: constitution.congress.gov (Amdt26 overview); britannica.com/topic/Twenty-sixth-Amendment

questions.q: It was written by James Madison and proposed alongside the Bill of Rights, yet a college student's term paper revived it and it became law more than 200 years later. Which amendment is it?
questions.detail: One amendment in the Constitution was written by James Madison and proposed alongside the Bill of Rights, yet did not become law until a college student's term paper revived it more than 200 years later.
questions.why: It sounds like an old amendment, so people guess a low number. It is actually the newest: the 27th, on congressional pay, proposed 1789, ratified 1992.
questions.more: Gregory Watson got a C on the paper arguing it was still ratifiable, then led the campaign that finished it.
questions.source: constitution.congress.gov (Amdt27 overview); congress.gov/crs-product/LSB10933

questions.q: Congress proposes amendments with two-thirds, but ratification takes three-fourths of the states. With 50 states today, how many must say yes for an amendment to become law?
questions.detail: Once Congress sends an amendment to the states, a certain number of states must say yes before it becomes part of the Constitution. With 50 states today, that's the count.
questions.why: People remember 'two-thirds' for Congress and confuse the fractions. Ratification needs three-fourths of states, which is 38 of 50.
questions.more: The proposing step needs two-thirds of each house of Congress.
questions.source: archives.gov/federal-register/constitution; constitution.findlaw.com/article5

questions.q: Look at every U.S. paper bill in circulation. People assume only presidents appear on money, but how many of those faces never served as president?
questions.detail: Look at the faces on every U.S. paper bill in circulation. This is how many of those people never served as president.
questions.why: People assume only presidents go on money. Two do not: Alexander Hamilton (\$10) and Benjamin Franklin (\$100).
questions.more: A 1920s commission preferred presidents, but kept these two founders.
questions.source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States_on_currency; gilderlehrman.org (Hamilton on the \$10 bill)

questions.q: The Supreme Court first struck down an act of Congress as unconstitutional, inventing judicial review. People often guess the 1790s, but in which ten-year span did that ruling actually fall?
questions.detail: The Supreme Court first struck down an act of Congress as unconstitutional, inventing judicial review. Name the ten-year span that ruling fell in.
questions.why: People place this landmark vaguely 'early America' but often guess the 1790s. Marbury v. Madison was decided in 1803, the 1800s decade.
questions.more: Chief Justice John Marshall wrote the opinion.
questions.source: guides.loc.gov/marbury-v-madison; oyez.org/cases/1789-1850/5us137

questions.q: A unanimous Supreme Court ruled segregated public schools unconstitutional, overturning 'separate but equal.' Many tie this to the marches and guess too late, so name the decade.
questions.detail: A unanimous Supreme Court ruled that separate public schools for Black and white children were unconstitutional, overturning a 'separate but equal' precedent. Name the decade.
questions.why: Many tie this to the civil rights marches and guess the 1960s. Brown v. Board of Education was 1954, the 1950s.
questions.more: It overruled Plessy v. Ferguson (1896).
questions.source: archives.gov (Brown v. Board of Education); law.cornell.edu/wex/brown_v_board_of_education_(1954)

questions.q: Its nickname is a fact about the Constitution: by a unanimous convention vote in December 1787, which state was the very first to ratify it?
questions.detail: Its nickname is literally a fact about the Constitution: it was the very first state to ratify it, by a unanimous convention vote in December 1787.
questions.why: People guess a big or famous founding state like Virginia or Pennsylvania. Delaware ratified first, 30-0, on December 7, 1787.
questions.more: That is why its motto is 'The First State.'
questions.source: history.com/this-day-in-history/december-7/the-first-state; archives.delaware.gov (Ratification Document)

questions.q: People file the Wall Street crash that kicked off the Great Depression with the 1930s, but in which decade did Black Tuesday actually fall?
questions.detail: The Wall Street crash that kicked off the Great Depression happened on Black Tuesday, October 29, 1929.
questions.why: People file the crash with the Great Depression, which they picture as the 1930s. But the crash itself fell in the last autumn of the previous decade. The set is small and walkable (a dozen 20th-century decades), and the reflex-defeater is that the trigger and the long misery sit in different decades.
questions.more: The Dow lost about a quarter of its value over four trading days; the worst of the Depression came in the years that followed.
questions.source: History.com, 'Stock Market Crash of 1929'; Federal Reserve History, 'Stock Market Crash of 1929'

questions.q: Several western states let women vote first, but which state cast the 36th and deciding vote to ratify the 19th Amendment, when a young legislator changed his mind after a note from his mother?
questions.detail: Tennessee was the 36th and deciding state to ratify the 19th Amendment, giving women the vote in 1920.
questions.why: People expect a northern or western state to clinch women's suffrage, since several western states allowed women to vote first. Instead a southern state cast the final ratifying vote, decided when a young legislator changed his mind after a note from his mother. The set is the states; the slicer is 'last needed,' not 'first to allow.'
questions.more: Three-fourths of the 48 states were needed; Tennessee's vote got the amendment over the line in August 1920.
questions.source: National Archives, '19th Amendment'; NPS, 'Tennessee and the 19th Amendment'

questions.q: People link Rosa Parks loosely to Selma or Birmingham, but in which Alabama city did the 1955-56 bus boycott sparked by her arrest actually take place?
questions.detail: The 1955-56 bus boycott sparked by Rosa Parks' arrest took place in this city.
questions.why: Parks and the early civil-rights movement are often loosely associated with bigger names like Selma or Birmingham. The boycott that launched the movement and brought a young Martin Luther King Jr. to prominence was in the state capital, Montgomery. The set is major Southern cities; the defeater is picking the wrong famous Alabama city.
questions.more: The boycott lasted 381 days and ended after the Supreme Court upheld a ruling against bus segregation.
questions.source: Library of Congress, 'Rosa Parks: The Bus Boycott'; History.com, 'Rosa Parks ignites bus boycott'

questions.q: It's easy to anchor on the Apollo 11 crew of three, but how many test pilots made up NASA's first group of astronauts, announced in 1959?
questions.detail: NASA's first group of astronauts, announced in 1959, was the Mercury Seven.
questions.why: It's easy to confuse the size of the first astronaut class with later, larger groups, or to anchor on famous numbers like the Apollo 11 crew of three. The original class was exactly seven test pilots. The set is small counts; the defeater is mixing up 'first astronauts' with a specific mission crew.
questions.more: They included Alan Shepard, the first American in space, and John Glenn, the first American to orbit Earth.
questions.source: NASA, 'Mercury Seven Astronaut Biographies'; Space.com, 'The Mercury 7 Astronauts'

questions.q: We reflexively say 50 states, but when Arizona joined in 1912 and completed the contiguous map, how many states were in the Union?
questions.detail: When Arizona joined in 1912, this was the total number of states in the Union.
questions.why: People know there are 50 states and may not register that the count sat at a stable number for almost half a century. Arizona completed the contiguous map at 48, where it stayed until 1959. The set is plausible state counts; the defeater is reflexively saying 50.
questions.more: New Mexico became the 47th state weeks earlier in 1912; Alaska and Hawaii pushed the total to 50 in 1959.
questions.source: National Archives, 'New Mexico and Arizona Statehood'; Legal Legacy, 'Arizona Joins the Union as the 48th State'

questions.q: It's the worst pandemic most people can name before COVID, so many misplace it later, but in which decade did the influenza pandemic that killed about 675,000 Americans strike?
questions.detail: The influenza pandemic that killed about 675,000 Americans struck in 1918-19.
questions.why: Because it's the worst pandemic most people can name before COVID, many misplace it later — guessing the 1920s or 1930s. It actually peaked in the final year of World War I, in the 1910s. The set is the decades; the slicer is tying it to the war's end rather than the Jazz Age.
questions.more: It killed more Americans than U.S. combat deaths in World War I, World War II, Korea, and Vietnam combined.
questions.source: CDC, 'History of 1918 Flu Pandemic'; Newsweek, 'How Many Americans Died From Spanish Flu'

questions.q: People bundle civil-rights landmarks into the 1960s, but in which decade did the Supreme Court rule school segregation unconstitutional in Brown v. Board of Education?
questions.detail: The Supreme Court ruled school segregation unconstitutional in Brown v. Board of Education in 1954.
questions.why: People bundle civil-rights landmarks into the 1960s alongside the big marches and laws. But the ruling that outlawed 'separate but equal' in schools came earlier, in the mid-1950s. The set is the decades; the defeater is that the legal turning point predates the decade most associated with the movement.
questions.more: The unanimous ruling overturned the 1896 Plessy v. Ferguson decision and helped ignite the wider movement.
questions.source: National Archives, 'Brown v. Board of Education (1954)'; Justia, 'Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka'

questions.q: The Soviet Union dissolved in 1991, so people round the Wall's fall up, but in which decade did the Berlin Wall actually fall?
questions.detail: The Berlin Wall fell in November 1989, a symbolic end to the Cold War.
questions.why: Because the Soviet Union dissolved in 1991 and reunification dragged on, people round the Wall's fall up to the 1990s. It actually happened in the closing weeks of the 1980s. The set is the decades; the slicer is separating the Wall's fall from the events that followed it.
questions.more: The Wall had stood 28 years; Germany formally reunified in October 1990.
questions.source: EBSCO Research Starters, 'Fall of the Berlin Wall'; History.com, 'Berlin Wall'

questions.q: Many assume so sweeping a program came in the postwar boom or the Great Society, but in which decade was the Social Security Act, creating old-age insurance, signed?
questions.detail: The Social Security Act, creating old-age insurance, was signed in 1935.
questions.why: Many assume so sweeping a federal program came during the postwar boom or the Great Society of the 1960s. It was actually a New Deal response to the Depression, signed in the mid-1930s. The set is the decades; the slicer is tying it to Depression relief rather than later prosperity.
questions.more: It created old-age insurance plus a federal-state unemployment system, funded by payroll taxes.
questions.source: U.S. House History, 'The Social Security Act of 1935'; History.com, 'Social Security Act'

questions.q: People focus on the speaker and forget the setting, or confuse it with Selma or Atlanta, but in which city was the 'I Have a Dream' speech delivered at the 1963 march?
questions.detail: The 1963 March for Jobs and Freedom, where 'I Have a Dream' was delivered, took place here.
questions.why: The 'Dream' speech is so iconic that people focus on the speaker and forget the venue, or confuse it with Selma or Atlanta. It was delivered from the Lincoln Memorial steps in the nation's capital. The set is major cities; the defeater is recalling the speech but not the setting.
questions.more: About 250,000 people attended; the march helped build pressure for the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
questions.source: Britannica, 'March on Washington'; History.com, 'King delivers I Have a Dream speech'
